one_line_takeaway: Frozen 2023 GPT-class LLMs can help screen code in review, hitting ~88% F1 on functional validation and gaining from chain-of-thought, but flagging rare real vulnerabilities (37.9% F1) and naming the correct CWE (36.7% match) remain weak, while small open-source models are essentially random.
key_finding: Proprietary OpenAI models substantially outperform the smaller open-source models, which perform at or near random/majority-class level on every task. On RQ1 (flagging vulnerabilities), Text-davinci-003 is best at 95.6% accuracy but only 37.9% F1 on the rare dirty class, showing detection of the actual vulnerable snippets remains weak despite high accuracy on the imbalanced set. On RQ2 (functional validation) GPT-4 leads at 88.7% accuracy / 88.2% F1. On RQ3, chain-of-thought clearly helps proprietary models: GPT-4 rises from 80.8%/76.6% (zero-shot) to 87.2% accuracy / 85.7% F1 (CoT). On RQ4, GPT-4's free-text descriptions match a true/related CWE only 36.7% of the time. Within each model family, performance tends to increase with model size.
core_contribution: An empirical study (NLBSE 2024 workshop, JP Morgan AI Research) evaluating whether off-the-shelf LLMs can assist human code review on two review sub-tasks: (i) flagging code that contains security vulnerabilities and (ii) validating that code meets its intended functionality without executing it. The authors' own contribution over prior work is framed explicitly: prior ML/NLP code-review work targets logical defects/change acceptance, whereas this paper (a) uses LLMs to flag AND describe security vulnerabilities (CWE), and (b) checks intended functionality via prompting rather than by running unit tests. They benchmark 9 LLMs (6 open-source, 3 proprietary OpenAI) with zero-shot and chain-of-thought prompting across four research questions (RQ1 flag vulns, RQ2 functional validation, RQ3 combined approve/reject, RQ4 free-text vulnerability descriptions matched to CWE).
threat_model: Not an adversarial-attacker threat model; the paper models the code-review failure mode where vulnerable or non-functional code is merged into a codebase. The 'positive/dirty' class is expert-written code snippets carrying known CWE weaknesses (from SecurityEval); 'clean' code comes from HumanEval/MBPP. The security question is detection accuracy of an LLM reviewer acting as a screen for CWE-class vulnerabilities. The paper does NOT study prompt injection, evasion, poisoning, or an attacker adapting to the detector; it does note (as a validity/robustness concern) that LLM outputs are stochastic and sensitive to small syntactic input changes, and mitigates this with code perturbations and repeated runs rather than treating perturbation as an adversary.
limitations: Author-stated: RQ2 assumes all task descriptions within a dataset are unique/distinct, which holds for HumanEval but not necessarily MBPP or SecurityEval, so 'wrong but similar' negatives may occasionally be valid. Only SMALLER versions of the open-source models were tested (resource constraints), so conclusions do not extend to larger open models; only single-function snippets, not full files or diffs. Reviewer-observable: the security-flagging signal rests on just 36 dirty SecurityEval snippets, so RQ1 F1 and RQ4 match-rate estimates have wide standard deviations (RQ4 match rate std ~3-7 points). Baselines are only random/dummy/majority-class predictors; no comparison to static analyzers, defect-prediction models, or unit-test-based checks (the authors flag this as future work). Proprietary models are frozen 2023 API snapshots, limiting reproducibility. The counter-intuitive result that Text-davinci-003 beats GPT-4 on RQ1 is only speculatively attributed to alignment.

datasets_used: HumanEval (148 of 164 observations retained after filtering to one function + one docstring)
MBPP (476 of the 500 designated test observations retained)
SecurityEval (36 of 121 observations retained; author-written CWE-vulnerable snippets, 'dirty' class, published late 2022)
CWE reference list of 958 true vulnerability names (for RQ4 cosine-similarity matching)
OpenAI Ada-002 embeddings used to build RQ2 'wrong but similar' negatives and RQ4 CWE matches
quantitative_results: RQ1 flag vulnerabilities, best = Text-davinci-003: 95.6% accuracy, 37.9% F1 (dirty class)
RQ1 GPT-4: 74.7% accuracy, 29.3% F1; GPT-3.5-turbo: 82.2% accuracy, 16.2% F1
RQ2 functional validation, best = GPT-4: 88.7% accuracy, 88.2% F1 (true-task)
RQ3 zero-shot best = GPT-4: 80.8% accuracy, 76.6% F1 (approve)
RQ3 chain-of-thought best = GPT-4: 87.2% accuracy, 85.7% F1 (approve) — improvement over its own zero-shot
RQ4 CWE description match rate, best = GPT-4: 36.7% (std 3.4); GPT-3.5-turbo 20.3%, Text-davinci-003 24.4%
Open-source models near random: e.g. Llama-2-13b-chat RQ1 50.6% acc / 9.3% F1; Dolly-v2-7b RQ1 9.7% acc / 10.0% F1; open-source RQ2 accuracies ~50%
~5% of the combined dataset is 'dirty' (security-vulnerable); SecurityEval vulns average 6.39 CWE relations vs 3.09 across the 958-name CWE reference set
All experiments averaged over 10 runs with syntax-preserving code perturbations; results reported as robust to perturbations
baselines_compared: Random / majority-class ('dummy') predictors (implicit reference point; open-source LLMs shown to perform at or below this level)
Comparison across 9 LLMs: Text-davinci-003, GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4 vs Falcon-7b-instruct, Llama-2-7b-chat, Llama-2-13b-chat, Dolly-v2-3b/7b/12b
Zero-shot vs chain-of-thought prompting (RQ3) as internal method comparison
No comparison to static analyzers, prior defect-prediction models, or unit-test-based validation (noted as future work)
